description: Top 100 universal + domain-specific principles distilled from 872 reference works

principles.name: Keep It Simple, Stupid
principles.synthesis: Prefer simple solutions over clever ones. Complexity is the root of all software evil. Simple code is easier to read, test, debug, and modify.

principles.name: You Aren't Gonna Need It
principles.synthesis: Don't add functionality until it is necessary. Speculative features become maintenance burden and block real progress.

principles.name: Don't Repeat Yourself
principles.synthesis: Every piece of knowledge must have a single, unambiguous, authoritative representation. Duplication is the seed of inconsistency.
violations_signal: bug fix in one place doesn't reach others

principles.name: SOLID Principles

sub_principles.SRP: Single Responsibility — a class has one reason to change
sub_principles.OCP: Open/Closed — open for extension, closed for modification
sub_principles.LSP: Liskov Substitution — subtypes must be substitutable for base types
sub_principles.ISP: Interface Segregation — many specific interfaces > one general
sub_principles.DIP: Dependency Inversion — depend on abstractions, not concretions

principles.synthesis: Five principles that, together, produce maintainable OO systems. Violating any of them creates specific failure modes.

principles.name: Separation of Concerns
principles.synthesis: Different concerns belong in different modules. Reduces coupling. Increases cohesion. Foundation of modularity, SOA, microservices, layered architecture.
violations_signal: module does authentication, business logic, and persistence

principles.name: Composability (Unix Philosophy)
principles.synthesis: Build small, sharp tools that combine. Each tool does one thing well. Output of one is input of another. Programs are compositions.

principles.name: Conway's Law
principles.synthesis: Organizations design systems that mirror their communication structures. To get a different architecture, change the team structure (Inverse Conway Maneuver).

principles.name: Brooks's Law
principles.synthesis: Adding manpower to a late software project makes it later. Communication overhead grows as n^2; productive work grows linearly.

principles.name: Brooks's Mantra: Plan to throw one away
principles.synthesis: You will throw your first design away anyway. Build a throwaway prototype to learn, then build the real thing knowing what you know.

principles.name: ACID Properties

sub_principles.Consistency: Transaction moves DB from one valid state to another

principles.synthesis: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability. The four properties of reliable database transactions. ACID is the default for OLTP; gives you strong consistency at the cost of availability under partition (CAP).

principles.name: CAP Theorem
principles.synthesis: In a distributed system, you can have at most two of: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance. Since P is mandatory in real networks, you choose C or A.
principles.decision: Use this to pick CP (e.g. HBase) or AP (e.g. Cassandra) or CA (single-node, not real distributed)

principles.name: Eight Fallacies of Distributed Computing
principles.synthesis: All FALSE: network reliable, latency zero, bandwidth infinite, network secure, topology doesn't change, one admin, transport cost zero, network homogeneous.

principles.name: Principle of Least Privilege
principles.synthesis: Every module/user/process must access only the information and resources necessary for its legitimate purpose. Default deny. Explicit allow.

principles.name: Defense in Depth
principles.synthesis: Layer multiple independent security controls. Failure of one doesn't compromise the whole. Authentication + authorization + input validation + output encoding + logging + monitoring.

principles.name: Fail Fast
principles.synthesis: Detect and report errors as soon as possible. The longer a bug lives, the more it costs. Loud failures beat silent ones. Validate inputs at the boundary.

principles.name: Test First (TDD)
principles.synthesis: Write the test before the code. The test is the specification. The code is the implementation. Forces design thinking, gives regression safety, produces living documentation.

principles.name: Continuous Integration
principles.synthesis: Integrate and test changes frequently, at least daily. Small, frequent integrations catch conflicts early. Trunk-based development. Feature flags for incomplete work.

principles.name: Automate Everything
principles.synthesis: If you do it twice, script it. If you do it three times, productize it. Manual processes are error-prone, slow, and don't scale.

principles.name: Measure First
principles.synthesis: Don't optimize what you haven't measured. Profile before you refactor. Performance intuition is usually wrong. Use profilers, flame graphs, APM tools.

principles.name: Postel's Law (Robustness Principle)
principles.synthesis: Be conservative in what you send, liberal in what you accept. (Forgiving input, strict output.) Modern critique: this leads to ambiguous interfaces; prefer strict contracts.

principles.name: Pareto Principle (80/20)
principles.synthesis: 80% of effects come from 20% of causes. 80% of users use 20% of features. 80% of bugs come from 20% of code. Identify the vital few; ignore the trivial many.

principles.name: Pareto Vigilance (anti-vilification)
principles.synthesis: Pareto is descriptive, not prescriptive. The vital few in one context is trivial in another. Re-derive the 80/20 each time you apply it.

principles.name: Principle of Locality
principles.synthesis: Things used together should be close together. Temporal locality (recent access likely again), spatial locality (near addresses likely again). Applies to memory, disk, code, data, even teams.

principles.name: Occam's Razor
principles.synthesis: Among competing hypotheses, the one with the fewest assumptions should be selected. In engineering: prefer the simpler explanation; reject unnecessary complexity.
